topic: A person accidentally sends a text to the wrong recipient
raw_output:
[story 1]

The screen lit up, a stark rectangle in the dim light of the late-night diner. Liam had meant to send a quick, silly meme to his friend Mark, but his thumb had slipped, landing squarely on the contact labeled 'Sarah - Work.' A cold knot tightened in his stomach. He stared at the message: "\[Meme attached]." His breath hitched, a shallow, quick intake of air. He watched the little 'Delivered' notification flicker, then the dreaded 'Read' icon appear moments later. He fumbled for his phone, his fingers suddenly clumsy, the plastic feeling slick and alien in his grip. He couldn't look away from the message thread, the silence of the diner suddenly amplifying the frantic drumming in his chest.

[story 2]

Maya reread the text three times, the words swimming before her eyes. It was supposed to be a joke, a harmless slip-up. Instead, the recipient was a number she didn't recognize, a name that felt vaguely familiar but utterly wrong. A tremor ran through her hand as she tried to delete the message, but the system was stubbornly refusing. She leaned back in her chair, the cheap vinyl squeaking under her weight. Her gaze darted toward the entrance of the diner, as if expecting someone to materialize and confirm the strange intrusion. A bead of sweat traced a path down her temple. Every small sound—the clatter of dishes, the hum of the refrigerator—seemed amplified, pressing in on her.

[story 3]

"Oh, blast it all," whispered David, his voice sounding unnaturally high in the quiet room. He had intended that message for his sister, a casual update about his weekend plans. Now, it was going to someone entirely unknown. He pressed his forehead against the cool glass of the phone, trying to steady his breathing. His knuckles were white where he gripped the device. He kept glancing over his shoulder, convinced that the very air in the small space had shifted, becoming heavier, charged with an unseen tension. He waited, rigid, for some reaction that never came, the silence stretching into an uncomfortable, protracted moment.
